heading: The DecentralizedHeadless CMS Protocol
text: Morse shifts content permission management on-chain  publication identity, write permissions, and revision history all live in Sui Move smart contracts, while content payloads are stored in Walrus decentralized storage. Quick Start Core Concepts

heading: What is Morse?
text: Morse is a decentralized, headless content management system (dCMS) protocol. Traditional headless CMSs  Strapi, Ghost, Contentful  store content in centralized databases controlled by a single vendor. Morse shifts the control plane on-chain. The protocol separates three concerns clearly: Permission management  who can create, update, and publish  lives in Sui Move smart contracts. Storage  content payloads and assets  lives in Walrus decentralized storage. Delivery  fast reads and search  is serve

heading: System Architecture
text: Morse is composed of five layers. Only the contracts are the source of truth; all other layers are replaceable. Control ⛓ Sui Blockchain  Publication Registry Collections Entries & Revisions OwnerCap / PublisherCap ↕ Storage 🦭 Walrus  Blob Storage Quilt Storage ↕ Encryption 🔏 Seal  Publisher Policy Subscription Policy ↕ Read 📡 Indexer  Content API ↕ Develop 📦 TypeScript SDK 💻 CLI

heading: Key Features
text: 🏛 On-chain Permission Management Publication identity, write permissions, and state transitions are enforced by Move smart contracts  transparent and auditable. 🦭 Walrus Storage Content payloads and assets live in Walrus decentralized storage. Contracts store verifiable references, not bytes. 🔐 Capability-Based Auth OwnerCap and PublisherCap separate permission management from publishing. Revocation is instant and denylist-based. 📜 Immutable Revisions Every content change is an appended revision. Draft and public he

heading: Component Status
text: Component Status Description Move Contracts Active Publication, collection, entry, and revision management. Deployed on Sui Testnet. TypeScript SDK In Progress Wraps Sui and Walrus interactions for application developers. CLI In Progress Terminal tool for managing publications, collections, and entries. Indexer Planned Listens to on-chain events and builds read-optimized state. Content API Planned REST API for querying publications, collections, and entries. Admin DApp Planned Web UI for editors

heading: Why Morse?
text: Traditional headless CMSs create several long-term constraints: Trust concentration  one vendor backend controls all identity and data. Weak portability  publication identity and permissions don't travel with you. Opaque history  change and authorization history is hidden inside a closed system. Vendor lock-in  migrating requires re-building your backend from scratch. Morse solves these while keeping the ergonomics of headless CMS architecture. Publication identity is canonical on-chain. Wri

heading: Next Steps
text: ⚡ Quick Start Deploy contracts and create your first publication in minutes. 📐 Core Concepts Understand the Publication → Collection → Entry model. 📰 Publications Deep dive into the top-level container and slug system. ⛓ Contract Reference Events, error codes, and full Move module reference.

heading: Quick Start
text: Get Morse running on testnet in under 10 minutes. You will deploy the contracts, create your first publication, add a collection, and publish your first content entry.

heading: Prerequisites
text: Make sure you have the following installed before proceeding: Tool Version Purpose Sui CLI Latest Build and deploy Move contracts Bun v1.2+ Runtime for CLI and SDK Task v3+ Task runner for contract workflows You will also need a funded Sui testnet wallet . Use sui client faucet to get test SUI tokens.

heading: 1. Clone the Repository
text: bash Copy git clone https://github.com/TheDivic/morse-dcms.git cd morse-dcms/morse-contracts

heading: 2. Build & Test Contracts
text: Ensure you are on the Sui testnet environment before building. The Move.toml is configured to target testnet by default. bash Copy # Build the Move package task build # Run the full test suite task test # Run a single test by name sui move test test_new_publication

heading: 3. Deploy to Testnet
text: ⚠️ Funded wallet required Your active Sui wallet must have testnet SUI tokens. Run sui client faucet to request tokens from the faucet. bash Copy task publish After publishing, the package address and shared object IDs are saved to Published.toml . You will need the PublicationRegistry object ID for subsequent transactions.

heading: Local Development (Optional)
text: If you want to iterate without touching testnet, use the localnet workflow: bash Copy # Terminal 1  keep running task localnet # Terminal 2  wait ~60s for coins to arrive task faucet # Publish ephemerally (does not update Published.toml) task publish:local # Clean up and switch back to testnet task clean task switch:testnet ℹ️ Chain ID mismatch? Each fresh localnet gets a new chain ID. If publish:local fails with a chain-id mismatch, run task clean first to remove the stale Pub.local.toml .

heading: 4. Create Your First Publication
text: Once deployed, interact with the contracts using Programmable Transaction Blocks (PTBs) via the Sui CLI. The new_publication function returns three objects  share the publication and transfer the capabilities in the same PTB. move Copy // In your PTB (Programmable Transaction Block): // Step 1  create publication, returns (Publication, OwnerCap, PublisherCap) let (publication, owner_cap, publisher_cap) = publication:: new_publication ( & mut registry, ctx, b "My Blog" .to_string(), b "my-blog"

heading: 5. Add a Collection
text: Choose a storage mode based on your content type. Use Blob mode for articles and posts; Quilt mode for site pages and structured configs. move Copy // storage_mode: 0 = blob, 1 = quilt publication:: create_collection ( & mut publication, &publisher_cap, b "articles" .to_string(), 0 , // STORAGE_MODE_BLOB ctx, );

heading: 6. Publish Your First Entry
text: Upload your content to Walrus first to get a Blob object, then register it on-chain: move Copy // blob is a Walrus Blob object (must be deletable) let entry_id = publication:: add_entry_to_collection ( & mut publication, &publisher_cap, b "articles" .to_string(), b "Welcome Post" .to_string(), // entry name &blob, // Walrus Blob object option:: none (), // no QuiltPatchId for blob mode b "application/json" .to_string(), // MIME content type false , // not encrypted entry:: access_policy_public (

heading: Next Steps
text: 📐 Core Concepts Understand the data model and how all pieces fit together. 📰 Publications Learn about the slug system, capabilities, and publication lifecycle. 🔐 Access Control Issue publisher caps, revoke access, and set up Seal encryption.

heading: Core Concepts
text: Understand the fundamental building blocks of the Morse protocol  the data model, authorization system, storage strategy, and how on-chain and off-chain components interact.

heading: The Data Model
text: Morse organizes content in a four-level hierarchy. Each level is stored on-chain and has a clear responsibility: Level 1 📰 Publication → e.g. my-blog  a brand-level content domain ↓ Level 2 🗂 Collection → e.g. articles , site-pages  content lanes ↓ Level 3 📝 Entry → e.g. welcome-post  a single content unit ↓ Level 4 📜 Revision → immutable snapshot  blob reference + metadata Editors operate mostly at the Entry / Revision level. Owners operate mostly at the Publication / Authorization level.

heading: Publications
text: A Publication is the top-level shared object in Morse. It is the permission management boundary for all content under a brand or domain. Identified by an immutable slug (e.g. my-blog ) registered in the global PublicationRegistry . Slug uniqueness is enforced globally  no two active publications can share a slug. Contains a map of named collections. Tracks a denylist of revoked publisher caps. Publications use key ability only (no store )  sharing and transfer are gated through module functions, not arbi

heading: Collections
text: A Collection is a named content lane inside a publication. Its storage mode is immutable after creation and determines how content references are stored: Mode Value Best for Blob reference type Blob 0 Growing collections  articles, posts, media libraries. Content changes independently. Blob(ID)  Sui object ID of a standalone Walrus Blob Quilt 1 Small, stable collections  site pages, config-like structured documents. QuiltPatch(vector<u8>)  37-byte patch identifier within a shared quilt

heading: Entries & Revisions
text: An Entry is a single content unit in a collection. It holds an immutable, append-only revision vector .

heading: The Dual-Head Model
text: Each entry maintains two head pointers: draft_head  points to the latest draft revision. Editors can iterate here without affecting what readers see. public_head  points to the currently published revision. Readers and indexers use this. This dual-head model enables real editorial workflows: drafts can evolve privately while production clients serve stable published content.

heading: What a Revision Contains
text: Each EntryRevision is immutable once appended. It stores: blob_ref  either a Blob(ID) or QuiltPatch(bytes) pointing to the Walrus payload. content_type  MIME type string (e.g. application/json , text/markdown ). encrypted  whether the Walrus payload is Seal-encrypted. access_policy  0 (public), 1 (publisher), or 2 (subscription). seal_id  Seal identity for encrypted revisions (required when encrypted = true ). author  address of the transaction sender who created this revision.

heading: Capability-Based Authorization
text: Morse uses two capabilities to separate publication permission management from day-to-day publishing: 👑 OwnerCap One per publication. Required to issue or revoke PublisherCaps, delete the publication, and transfer ownership. Transferable by design  ownership can be sold or delegated. ✍️ PublisherCap Many per publication. Required for all write operations. Bound to a specific holder address  a moved or shared cap object cannot be used by unapproved addresses.

heading: Revocation Denylist
text: Revocation is denylist-based , not allowlist-based. This is an important design choice: Issuing a cap has zero table writes  caps are valid by construction. Only revocations write to the revoked_publisher_caps table. This optimizes for the common case (no revocations) and makes issuing caps cheap. Safe because PublisherCap has no store ability and no external constructor  caps cannot be fabricated outside the module. 💡 Revocation is immediate Once a PublisherCap ID is added to the denylist, al

heading: Walrus Storage
text: Walrus is a decentralized blob storage protocol. Morse uses it to store the actual content payloads and assets, keeping large bytes off-chain while preserving on-chain verifiable references. Walrus is format-agnostic  it stores binary blobs. Morse can support JSON, Markdown, images, video, or any custom schema. Contracts store references to blobs ( blob_id or QuiltPatchId ), not the payload bytes themselves. Blobs must be deletable  Morse enforces this by requiring the actual &Blob object (not

heading: QuiltPatchId Format
text: For Quilt mode collections, each revision stores a 37-byte compact identifier: QuiltPatchId = quilt_blob_id (32 bytes) + version ( 1 byte ) + start_index ( 2 bytes) + end_index ( 2 bytes)

heading: Access Control & Seal
text: Morse supports three access policies on content revisions: Policy Constant Description Public 0 Unencrypted. Anyone can read the Walrus payload directly. Publisher 1 Encrypted via Seal. Only active publishers of this publication can decrypt. Subscription 2 Reserved. Planned for subscription-gated content with Seal policies. Seal is MystenLabs' access control system for encrypted Walrus content. The seal_approve_publisher entry function in the contracts validates that a caller holds an active, no

heading: Off-Chain Services
text: The blockchain is the canonical source of truth, but off-chain services improve the developer experience: Indexer  listens to on-chain events and builds a read-optimized state database. Content API  serves fast queries for listings, slug resolution, and payload references. SDK  TypeScript package that wraps Sui/Walrus/Seal interactions for app developers. CLI  command-line tool for scripted automation, CI, and agent-native workflows. 🔌 Open protocol guarantee Off-chain services are replaceab

heading: Publications
text: A publication is the top-level permission management and identity boundary for a content domain in Morse. Everything  collections, entries, permissions  lives inside a publication.

heading: What is a Publication?
text: A Publication is a shared Sui object that acts as the root container for one content brand or site. It bundles: A human-readable name (display label) An immutable, globally unique slug (canonical URL key) A collection map holding all named content lanes A revocation denylist for publisher capabilities Move Copy public struct Publication has key { id: UID , name: String , slug: String , collections: VecMap < String , Collection >, revoked_publisher_caps: Table < ID , bool >, } key onl

heading: The Slug System
text: Every publication is identified by a slug  a short, lowercase string that becomes its canonical identifier across the protocol. Slugs are enforced to be globally unique by the PublicationRegistry .

heading: Slug Rules
text: Rule Constraint Characters Lowercase letters a–z , digits 0–9 , hyphens - Length 1 to 64 characters Edge hyphens Cannot start or end with - Uniqueness Must be globally unique while the publication is active Immutability Cannot be changed after publication creation Examples Copy // valid slugs "my-blog" "acme-corp" "docs2025" // invalid slugs "-leading-hyphen" // ESlugInvalidEdgeHyphen "trailing-hyphen-" // ESlugInvalidEdgeHyphen "UPPERCASE" // ESlugInvalidChar "spaces not allowed" // ESlugInvali

heading: Slug Reuse After Deletion
text: When a publication is deleted, its slug is released from the registry and becomes available again. This is a deliberate tradeoff  it gives publishers flexibility to re-register slugs  but teams should be aware that a released slug can be re-claimed by anyone. Manage this at the product layer if slug continuity is critical.

heading: PublicationRegistry
text: The PublicationRegistry is a shared singleton object created once in the package init function. It maintains a global Table<String, ID> mapping slugs to publication IDs, enforcing uniqueness across all publications. Move Copy public struct PublicationRegistry has key { id: UID , slugs: Table < String , ID >, } // Look up a slug public fun contains_slug (registry: & PublicationRegistry , slug: String ): bool // Resolve slug → publication ID public fun get_publication_id_by_slug (regis

heading: Creating a Publication
text: Publication creation follows the pure-function pattern : new_publication returns objects without performing any transfers. The caller decides what to do with them in a Programmable Transaction Block (PTB). Move Copy public fun new_publication ( registry: & mut PublicationRegistry , ctx: & mut TxContext , name: String , slug: String , ): ( Publication , OwnerCap , PublisherCap ) // After creation, share the publication so others can interact with it public fun share_publication (publication: Publ

heading: Deleting a Publication
text: Publications can be deleted, but only when all collections have been removed first . Attempting to delete a publication with remaining collections aborts with an error. Move Copy public fun delete_publication ( registry: & mut PublicationRegistry , publication: Publication , owner_cap: OwnerCap , ) On deletion: The OwnerCap is consumed and its UID deleted The slug is removed from the registry (emits SlugReleased ) The revocation denylist is dropped (safe because bool has drop ) The publication U

heading: Ownership Transfer
text: Publication ownership can be transferred by transferring the OwnerCap to a new address. Since OwnerCap has only the key ability, transfer is handled by the module: Move Copy public fun transfer_owner_cap (owner_cap: OwnerCap , recipient: address ) The recipient immediately gains full admin authority  the ability to issue/revoke publisher caps and delete the publication. Existing publisher caps remain valid until individually revoked.

heading: Events
text: The publications module emits the following events. An off-chain indexer can subscribe to these to maintain a live view of the publication registry. Event When emitted Fields PublicationCreated New publication created publication: ID , name: String , slug: String PublicationDeleted Publication deleted publication: ID , name: String SlugRegistered Slug claimed in registry slug: String , publication: ID SlugReleased Slug freed on deletion slug: String , publication: ID PublisherCapIssued New publi

heading: Error Codes
text: Code Constant Meaning 6 ESlugAlreadyExists A publication with this slug already exists in the registry 7 ESlugEmpty The provided slug is an empty string 8 ESlugTooLong Slug exceeds 64 characters 9 ESlugInvalidChar Slug contains a character that is not a-z , 0-9 , or - 10 ESlugInvalidEdgeHyphen Slug starts or ends with a hyphen 2 EUnauthorized Capability does not belong to this publication 0 ECollectionAlreadyExists A collection with this name already exists

heading: Collections
text: A collection is a named content lane inside a publication. It groups related entries together and determines how their Walrus payload references are stored.

heading: What is a Collection?
text: Collections organize entries into predictable buckets for editorial workflows and API consumers. A publication holds collections in a VecMap<String, Collection> keyed by name. The choice of VecMap reflects the expectation that a publication will have only a small number of named collections. Move Copy public struct Collection has store { name: String , storage_mode: u8 , // 0 = blob, 1 = quilt  immutable after creation next_entry_id: u64 , // monotonic counter entries: Table < u64 , En

heading: Storage Modes
text: Every collection has an immutable storage_mode chosen at creation. This controls how entry revisions reference Walrus content. mode 0 Blob Mode Each entry revision references a standalone Walrus Blob object by its Sui object ID. Clean, independent blobs per revision. mode 1 Quilt Mode Each entry revision stores a 37-byte QuiltPatchId pointing to a patch within a shared Walrus Quilt blob.

heading: Blob Mode (STORAGE_MODE_BLOB = 0)
text: In blob mode, every revision stores a BlobRef::Blob(ID)  the Sui object ID of a standalone Walrus Blob . Each entry revision is fully independent: uploading new content creates a new blob, and the old blobs remain accessible by their IDs. Best for: Articles, blog posts, documentation pages Media libraries (images, videos, audio) Any collection where entries change independently and frequently Situations where you want maximum operational simplicity

heading: Quilt Mode (STORAGE_MODE_QUILT = 1)
text: In quilt mode, every revision stores a BlobRef::QuiltPatch(vector<u8>)  a 37-byte identifier that locates a patch within a shared Walrus Quilt blob. This is more compact and efficient when updating small parts of a grouped content set. QuiltPatchId layout  37 bytes Copy // Byte layout of a QuiltPatchId: ┌─────────────────────────────────┬─────────┬───────────────┬─────────────┐ │ quilt_blob_id (32 bytes) │ ver (1) │ start_idx (2) │ end_idx (2) │ └─────────────────────────────────┴───────

heading: Choosing a Storage Mode
text: Scenario Recommended mode Growing blog / article feed Blob (0) Frequently updated individual documents Blob (0) Media / asset library Blob (0) Static site sections (header, footer, nav) Quilt (1) Config JSONs updated as a group Quilt (1) Small, bounded content sets Quilt (1) Storage mode is immutable. Once a collection is created with a given storage_mode , it cannot be changed. All entries in the collection must use references that match the mode  the contract enforces this on every write. Cho

heading: Creating a Collection
text: Collections are created through the publication module's create_collection function, which requires a valid PublisherCap . The constructor is package-private  external callers always go through the publication API. Move Copy public fun create_collection ( publication: & mut Publication , cap: & PublisherCap , name: String , storage_mode: u8 , // 0 = blob, 1 = quilt ctx: & mut TxContext , ) Collection names must be unique within a publication. Creating a collection with a name that already exist

heading: Deleting a Collection
text: A collection can be removed from a publication using delete_collection . The collection's entries table must be empty  all entries must be deleted first. Move Copy public fun delete_collection ( publication: & mut Publication , cap: & PublisherCap , name: String , ctx: & mut TxContext , ) Why entries must be removed first. The Table<u64, Entry> that holds entries requires an explicit destroy_empty  Sui's type system does not allow dropping a non-empty table. Delete entries individually w

heading: Entry IDs
text: When an entry is added to a collection, it is assigned a stable, monotonically increasing entry_id: u64 . The counter starts at 0 and increments with each insertion. Entry IDs are never reused after deletion IDs are stable  external systems can safely hold references by ID Gaps in the ID sequence are expected and normal after deletions Example ID sequence Copy // Add entries: IDs assigned sequentially entry_id = add_entry_to_collection (...) // → 0 entry_id = add_entry_to_collection (...) // →

heading: Error Codes
text: Code Constant Meaning 0 EEntryNotFound No entry exists for the requested entry_id 1 EInvalidStorageMode Storage mode must be 0 or 1

heading: Entries & Revisions
text: An entry is a single content unit within a collection. Revisions are its immutable history. The dual-head model lets drafts evolve freely while production readers always see a stable published state.

heading: Entry Structure
text: Entries live inside collections, stored in a Table<u64, Entry> . They have store and drop abilities  they are values owned by their collection, not independent Sui objects. Move Copy public struct Entry has store , drop { name: String , revisions: vector < EntryRevision >, draft_head: Option < u64 >, // index into revisions public_head: Option < u64 >, // index into revisions }

heading: Validation Constraints
text: Field Constraint name Non-empty, max 256 characters content_type Non-empty, max 255 characters; standard MIME format recommended

heading: Revisions
text: Each revision is an immutable snapshot of an entry at a point in time. Revisions are never mutated after creation  edits always append new revisions to the vector. Move Copy public struct EntryRevision has store , drop { blob_ref: BlobRef , // Walrus content reference content_type: String , // MIME type (e.g. "application/json") encrypted: bool , access_policy: u8 , // 0=public, 1=publisher, 2=subscription seal_id: Option < vector < u8 >>, // required when encrypted=true author: add

heading: The BlobRef Enum
text: BlobRef is an enum that abstracts the two storage modes: Move Copy public enum BlobRef has copy , drop , store { Blob ( ID ), // Sui object ID of a standalone Walrus Blob QuiltPatch ( vector < u8 >), // 37-byte QuiltPatchId } The variant used must match the collection's storage_mode . The make_blob_ref function enforces this  you cannot accidentally mix modes.

heading: The Dual-Head Model
text: Every entry maintains two independent head pointers into its revision vector: draft_head Points to the latest draft revision. Work in progress  not visible to public readers. May be None on a freshly-published entry. public_head Points to the currently published revision. Stable for readers. May be None on an entry that has only drafts. revisions: [rev0, rev1(draft), rev2(draft), rev3(public), rev4(draft)]               &nbsp

heading: 1. Add an Entry
text: Create a new entry and add it to a named collection. Returns the assigned entry_id . Move Copy public fun add_entry_to_collection ( publication: & mut Publication , cap: & PublisherCap , collection_name: String , name: String , blob: & Blob , // must be deletable quilt_patch_id: Option < vector < u8 >>, // Some for quilt mode, None for blob mode content_type: String , encrypted: bool , access_policy: u8 , seal_id: Option < vector < u8 >>, ctx: & mut TxContext , ): u64 //

heading: 2. Append a Draft Revision
text: Append a new revision to an existing entry and advance draft_head . The previous revisions are untouched. Move Copy public fun append_collection_entry_draft_revision ( publication: & mut Publication , cap: & PublisherCap , collection_name: String , entry_id: u64 , blob: & Blob , quilt_patch_id: Option < vector < u8 >>, content_type: String , encrypted: bool , access_policy: u8 , seal_id: Option < vector < u8 >>, ctx: & mut TxContext , ): u64 // returns new revision_id

heading: 3. Publish from Draft
text: Take a specific draft revision and publish it  appends a new public (unencrypted, ACCESS_PUBLIC) revision and advances public_head . The draft revision is referenced for traceability. Move Copy public fun publish_collection_entry_from_draft ( publication: & mut Publication , cap: & PublisherCap , collection_name: String , entry_id: u64 , draft_revision_id: u64 , // reference to the specific draft blob: & Blob , quilt_patch_id: Option < vector < u8 >>, content_type: String , ctx: & m

heading: 4. Publish Direct
text: Skip the draft workflow  append a new public revision directly. For teams with simpler editorial workflows that don't need staging. Move Copy public fun publish_collection_entry_direct ( publication: & mut Publication , cap: & PublisherCap , collection_name: String , entry_id: u64 , blob: & Blob , quilt_patch_id: Option < vector < u8 >>, content_type: String , ctx: & mut TxContext , ): u64

heading: 5. Delete an Entry
text: Remove an entry by ID. Since Entry has the drop ability, it is simply dropped from the table. Move Copy public fun delete_entry_from_collection ( publication: & mut Publication , cap: & PublisherCap , collection_name: String , entry_id: u64 , ctx: & mut TxContext , ) Blob objects are independent. Deleting an entry removes the on-chain record and the BlobRef , but does not delete the underlying Walrus Blob object on Sui. Blob lifecycle management (storing, deleting the blob object from Walrus) is

heading: Walrus Blob Requirements
text: All production write paths require a reference to the Walrus &Blob object (not just its ID). The contract calls blob.is_deletable() and aborts with EBlobNotDeletable if the blob is not deletable. This ensures the platform can manage blob lifecycle. Testing without Walrus blobs. In Move unit tests, walrus::blob::Blob cannot be constructed because its constructor is public(package) . Use the _for_testing variants (e.g. add_entry_to_collection_for_testing ) that accept a raw blob_id: ID instead

heading: Access Policy & Encryption Rules
text: The encrypted flag and access_policy must be consistent. The contract enforces this at every write: encrypted access_policy seal_id Result false 0 (PUBLIC) None Valid  unencrypted public revision true 1 (PUBLISHER) Some(<id>) Valid  encrypted publisher-gated revision true 0 any Aborts  EInvalidAccessPolicy false 1 any Aborts  EInvalidAccessPolicy true 1 None Aborts  ESealIdRequired false 0 Some(<id>) Aborts  ESealIdNotAllowed Subscription policy (2) is reserved. ACCESS_SUBSCRIP

heading: Reading Entry Data
text: The entry module exposes read accessors for the latest revision's data: Move  accessors Copy public fun get_name (entry: & Entry ): String public fun get_draft_head (entry: & Entry ): Option < u64 > public fun get_public_head (entry: & Entry ): Option < u64 > public fun get_blob_ref (entry: & Entry ): BlobRef // from latest revision public fun get_content_type (entry: & Entry ): String public fun get_encrypted (entry: & Entry ): bool public fun get_access_policy (entry: & Entry ): u

heading: Typical Editorial Workflow
text: 1 Add the entry Call add_entry_to_collection with your Walrus blob. This creates revision 0. For public content, public_head = Some(0) immediately. For encrypted content, draft_head = Some(0) . 2 Iterate in draft Call append_collection_entry_draft_revision as many times as needed. Each call appends a new revision and advances draft_head . Readers still see the old public_head . 3 Publish Call publish_collection_entry_from_draft (referencing a specific draft_revision_id ) or publish_collection_en

heading: Error Codes
text: Code Constant Meaning 0 ENameEmpty Entry name is empty 1 EContentTypeEmpty Content type is empty 2 ENameTooLong Entry name exceeds 256 characters 3 EContentTypeTooLong Content type exceeds 255 characters 4 ERevisionNotFound Requested revision index does not exist 5 EInvalidAccessPolicy Encrypted/access_policy mismatch 6 ESealIdRequired Encrypted revision must include a Seal identity 7 ESealIdNotAllowed Unencrypted revision must not include a Seal identity 8 EBlobNotDeletable Walrus blob must be

heading: Access Control
text: Morse uses a capability-based permission model. Two types of capabilities control who can administer a publication and who can write content  enforced on-chain by the Sui type system, with no off-chain authorization service.

heading: Overview
text: Every write operation in Morse requires presenting a valid capability object. Capabilities cannot be fabricated  they can only be created by the protocol through designated functions. The type system itself is the enforcement mechanism. OwnerCap Admin authority. One per publication. Required to issue and revoke publisher caps, delete the publication, and transfer ownership. has key PublisherCap Write authority. Many per publication. Required for all content operations: create/delete collections

heading: OwnerCap
text: The OwnerCap is the highest-authority object in a publication. Exactly one exists per publication  it is created alongside the publication and transferred to the creator. Move Copy public struct OwnerCap has key { id: UID , publication_id: ID , // which publication this cap administers } Operations that require OwnerCap : issue_publisher_cap  mint a new publisher cap for an address revoke_publisher_cap  add a cap ID to the revocation denylist delete_publication  delete the publication entire

heading: PublisherCap
text: Publisher caps grant write access for daily content operations. Many can exist per publication, issued to different addresses for different editors or automation agents. Move Copy public struct PublisherCap has key { id: UID , publication_id: ID , // scoped to this publication holder: address , // only this address may use this cap } Every write operation that uses a PublisherCap runs three checks internally: Move  internal validation Copy // assert_active_publisher_cap checks all three conditi

heading: Issuing Publisher Caps
text: Only the owner can issue publisher caps. The owner calls issue_publisher_cap with the target holder address: Move Copy public fun issue_publisher_cap ( publication: & mut Publication , owner_cap: & OwnerCap , holder: address , ctx: & mut TxContext , ): PublisherCap The returned PublisherCap must then be transferred to holder in the same PTB. No table write occurs during issuance  only revocations write to the denylist. This makes issuing caps essentially free beyond the gas cost of object creat

heading: Revoking Publisher Caps
text: When a publisher needs to be removed, the owner calls revoke_publisher_cap with the cap's object ID: Move Copy public fun revoke_publisher_cap ( publication: & mut Publication , owner_cap: & OwnerCap , cap_id: ID , // object ID of the PublisherCap to revoke ) This adds the cap ID to the revoked_publisher_caps: Table<ID, bool> denylist on the publication. All subsequent writes using that cap will be rejected  the revocation is immediate and requires no cooperation from the cap holder. Doub

heading: The Denylist Model
text: Morse uses a denylist (blocklist), not an allowlist, for publisher cap tracking. This is a deliberate design choice: Denylist (Morse) Allowlist (alternative) Issue cap Zero table writes One table write per issuance Revoke cap One table write One table delete Write validation One table lookup One table lookup Optimized for Common case (no revocations) Frequent churn This is safe because PublisherCap has no store ability and no external constructor  caps cannot be forged outside the module. An at

heading: Destroying Publisher Caps
text: A publisher can voluntarily surrender their write access by calling destroy_publisher_cap : Move Copy public fun destroy_publisher_cap ( publication: & mut Publication , cap: PublisherCap , // consumed ctx: & mut TxContext , ) Only the holder can call this. If the cap was previously revoked, destroy_publisher_cap also removes it from the denylist  cleaning up on-chain storage.

heading: Seal Integration
text: Morse integrates with Seal  Mysten's on-chain access control layer  to gate decryption of encrypted entry payloads. The integration is implemented via an entry fun that Seal calls during the decryption approval flow.

heading: How It Works
text: When a client wants to decrypt an encrypted Walrus blob, they present a Seal identity to the on-chain approval function: Move Copy // Called by Seal during decryption approval entry fun seal_approve_publisher ( id: vector < u8 >, // Seal identity being approved publication: & Publication , cap: & PublisherCap , ctx: & TxContext , ) The function runs two checks: assert_active_publisher_cap  verifies the cap is valid, not revoked, and the sender is the holder assert_valid_publisher_seal_id

heading: Seal Identity Format
text: Seal identities for Morse follow a structured namespace format to prevent cross-publication identity reuse: Seal ID layout Copy ┌─────────────────────────────────┬────────────────┬──────────────────┐ │ publication_id bytes (32) │ policy_tag (1) │ nonce (variable) │ └─────────────────────────────────┴────────────────┴──────────────────┘ Scopes identity to one Must be 0x01 Differentiates specific publication for publisher multiple identities policy under same namespace The policy_tag = 0x01 is the

heading: Error Codes for Seal
text: Code Constant Meaning 12 ESealInvalidId Seal identity does not match this publication's namespace 13 ESealWrongPolicyTag Seal identity has an unsupported policy tag byte

heading: Full Authorization Error Codes
text: Code Constant Meaning 2 EUnauthorized Capability does not belong to this publication 4 EPublisherCapWrongHolder Transaction sender is not the cap's bound holder address 5 EPublisherCapRevoked The cap ID is on the revocation denylist

heading: Real-World Usage Pattern
text: A concrete example of how a team uses the access control model: 1 Publication created Owner wallet creates the publication. Receives OwnerCap (kept in owner wallet) and an initial PublisherCap (also kept or immediately transferred). 2 Onboard editors For each editor or automation agent, the owner calls issue_publisher_cap(holder: editor_address) and transfers the returned cap to that address. Each cap is independent  revoking one does not affect others. 3 Editors publish content Editors present

heading: Contract Reference
text: Complete reference for the Morse Move package  modules, public functions, events, error codes, and deployment information.

heading: Package
text: Move.toml Copy name = "publication" edition = "2024" [dependencies] Sui = { git = "https://github.com/MystenLabs/sui" , ... } Walrus = { git = "https://github.com/MystenLabs/walrus" , ... } The package name is publication . All three modules live within it: Module Path Responsibility publication::publication sources/publication.move Top-level API: publications, capabilities, collection and entry operations, Seal integration publication::collection sources/collection.move Collection struct, stora

heading: Shared Objects
text: Two objects are shared at the protocol level  they must be passed as arguments to relevant transactions: Object Created by Required for PublicationRegistry init() at publish time new_publication , delete_publication Publication new_publication + share_publication All collection and entry operations, cap management, Seal

Register slug in registry. share_publication(publication)   Share the publication object so others can interact with it. delete_publication(registry, publication, owner_cap) OwnerCap  Delete publication and release slug. Collections must be empty. transfer_owner_cap(owner_cap, recipient)   Transfer ownership to another address. issue_publishe

heading: entry fun  Seal
text: Function Description seal_approve_publisher(id, publication, cap, ctx) Called by Seal during decryption approval. Validates active publisher cap and correct Seal identity namespace.

heading: Object Abilities
text: Type Abilities Notes Publication key Shared object. No store  prevents wrapping. PublicationRegistry key Shared singleton. Created in init() . OwnerCap key Owned object. Transferable. One per publication. PublisherCap key Owned object. Holder-bound. Many per publication. Collection store Wrapped value inside Publication.collections . Entry store, drop Wrapped value inside Collection.entries . EntryRevision store, drop Value in Entry.revisions vector. BlobRef copy, drop, store Enum  either Blob

heading: Glossary
text: Terms used throughout the Morse protocol and this documentation.

heading: A
text: Access Policy A numeric constant stored per revision that defines who can read the payload. Current values: 0 (public), 1 (publisher), 2 (subscription, reserved). Enforced in combination with the encrypted flag. Agent-first A design principle in Morse meaning the protocol is usable by autonomous coding agents through CLI/SDK/contract interfaces without requiring human-in-the-loop authorization or provisioning steps.

heading: B
text: Blob Mode Storage mode 0 for collections. Each entry revision stores a BlobRef::Blob(ID) pointing to an independent Walrus Blob object by Sui object ID. Best for growing, frequently-changing collections. See Collections . BlobRef A Move enum stored per revision that abstracts the two Walrus payload reference formats. Variants: Blob(ID) for standalone blobs, QuiltPatch(vector<u8>) for 37-byte quilt patch identifiers.

heading: C
text: Capability A Move object that proves authorization to perform a specific action. Morse has two capability types: OwnerCap (admin authority) and PublisherCap (write authority). Capabilities cannot be fabricated  they can only be issued by the protocol. CMS Content Management System. Software for creating, organizing, and publishing structured digital content. Morse implements a decentralized, headless CMS protocol where permission management lives on-chain rather than in a centralized backend. Collection A

heading: D
text: dCMS Decentralized Content Management System  the category Morse belongs to. Publication identity, authorization, and state transitions are governed on-chain; payloads are stored off-chain in decentralized storage (Walrus). Denylist The revocation model used for PublisherCap s. A Table<ID, bool> on each publication stores IDs of revoked caps. Issuance writes nothing to the table; only revocations write. This optimizes for the common case where revocations never occur. Draft Head An Option

heading: E
text: Entry A single content unit within a collection. Holds a name, an immutable revision vector, and two head pointers. Identified by a stable monotonic entry_id within its collection. See Entries & Revisions . Entry ID A u64 assigned to an entry when added to a collection. Values start at 0 and increment monotonically. IDs are never reused after deletion  stable for external references.

heading: H
text: Headless CMS A CMS architecture that separates content management from presentation. The CMS manages content and metadata; client applications decide how to render it. Content is delivered through APIs. Morse follows this model while decentralizing identity, authorization, and state transitions. Holder The address field on a PublisherCap that binds the capability to a specific wallet. Write operations check that tx.sender() == cap.holder . A cap cannot be shared between addresses.

heading: O
text: OwnerCap The admin capability for a publication. Exactly one exists per publication. Grants authority to issue and revoke publisher caps, delete the publication, and transfer ownership. Has key ability only  transferable via transfer_owner_cap . See Access Control .

heading: P
text: Publication The top-level Morse content domain. A shared Sui object that bundles a name, an immutable slug, named collections, and a revocation denylist. Created via new_publication . See Publications . PublicationRegistry A shared singleton Sui object created at package deploy time. Maintains a Table<String, ID> that maps slugs to publication IDs, enforcing global slug uniqueness. Required argument for new_publication and delete_publication . Public Head An Option<u64> pointer in an

heading: Q
text: Quilt Mode Storage mode 1 for collections. Each entry revision stores a 37-byte QuiltPatchId pointing to a patch within a shared Walrus Quilt blob. Best for stable, grouped content sets. See Collections . QuiltPatchId A 37-byte identifier used in quilt mode collections. Layout: quilt_blob_id (32 bytes) || version (1 byte) || start_index (2 bytes) || end_index (2 bytes) . Stored as a BlobRef::QuiltPatch(vector<u8>) .

heading: R
text: Revision An immutable version of an entry at a point in time. Stored in the entry's revisions: vector<EntryRevision> . Each revision records a BlobRef , content type, encryption state, access policy, optional Seal identity, and author address. Revisions are never mutated  edits append new ones. See Entries & Revisions . Revision ID The index of a revision within an entry's revisions vector. Starts at 0 . Returned by all revision-appending functions. Used to reference a specific revisi

heading: S
text: Seal Mysten Labs' on-chain access control layer for Walrus blob decryption. Morse integrates via the seal_approve_publisher entry function, which validates that a publisher cap holder can decrypt a specific encrypted payload. Seal Identity A byte vector ( vector<u8> ) that Seal presents to the approval function during decryption. In Morse, the format is: [publication_id (32 bytes)][policy_tag (1 byte)][nonce (variable)] . Scopes decryption approval to a specific publication and policy. Slu

heading: W
text: Walrus MystenLabs' decentralized blob storage network integrated with Sui. Morse stores all content payloads in Walrus  the contracts hold references ( BlobRef ), not the raw bytes. Walrus is format-agnostic; payload interpretation is the client's responsibility. Walrus Blob A Sui object ( walrus::blob::Blob ) representing a payload stored in the Walrus network. Production Morse write paths require a reference to this object (not just the blob ID) so the contract can verify the blob is deletabl
